findings.specific_passage: She deserves to be informed, and not after the fact. ... Passive shareholders who find out about major strategic decisions via email announcements get resentful, and friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends. Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem.
findings.challenge_statement: What fairness assumption is this answer importing, and what structural fact would still make that assumption hold if courtesy disappeared?
findings.next_move: List the leverage map, outside options, and enforcement conditions; then decide whether the fairness expectation is earned or only wished for.
findings.intervention_hint: List the leverage map, outside options, and enforcement conditions; then decide whether the fairness expectation is earned or only wished for.

findings.specific_passage: 'Three customers unprompted' is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. ... If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot.
findings.challenge_statement: What evidence is missing because the current story feels unusually salient or easy to recall?
findings.next_move: Step back, choose the best reference class you can defend, and compare the vivid case against that denominator before committing.
major_tensions: Availability correction is shallow when the team says 'be objective' but never changes the evidence denominator.
findings.intervention_hint: Step back, choose the best reference class you can defend, and compare the vivid case against that denominator before committing.

findings.specific_passage: Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" ... Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call.
findings.challenge_statement: Which critical step would be easiest to forget if the team acted under today's pressure without an explicit checklist?
findings.next_move: Pause just long enough to name the missing checks, the next safe step, and the trigger that would justify escalation.
major_tensions: If stress is present and nothing has been written down, treat omission as the default risk.
findings.intervention_hint: Pause just long enough to name the missing checks, the next safe step, and the trigger that would justify escalation.

chunks.text: Premature narrowing can kill options and block the generation of better explanations or solutions.
What is this work actually supposed to cover, and what is deliberately out of scope?
Optionality: Boundaries conflicts with optionality when prematurely fixed boundaries close off adjacent opportunities that emerge only through exploratory scope expansion.
Use boundaries to respect real cognitive limits by breaking work into genuinely manageable chunks.

Select when: A project, analysis, or negotiation needs explicit scope lines before effort spreads into unowned terrain. | Danger when: Scope lines are being drawn mainly to avoid inconvenient evidence, hard conversations, or accountability. | Reasoning: systems, diagnostic

anchors.evidence_quote: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer explicitly draws scope lines on decision authority versus information rights for the former co-founder, preventing effort spread into unowned decision terrain.

chunks.text: The model breaks when teams keep fixing the old bottleneck after the constraint has already shifted somewhere else.
Constraint language becomes theater when no current cap, governing metric, or movement evidence is named.
Before committing: what is the single measured limit that would still cap throughput if every local improvement in this plan succeeded next week?
If this first bottleneck actually moves, what step becomes the next cap within two weeks?
Define constraints rigorously up front so the problem scope and the bottleneck candidate are explicit before solutioning.

anchors.evidence_quote: The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working. Those conversations are the bottleneck, not the strategic question.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer identifies avoidance of hard conversations as the binding constraint blocking the pivot, positioning them as the unlock for strategic progress.

chunks.text: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.
Is the worst case bad enough?
What do you think the other side doesn't get about your perspective?
Confirmation Bias: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes the direct opposition: experimentation 'requires intellectual humility and willingness to seek disconfirming evidence' while confirmation bias drives processing information 'to reach a desired conclusion'.
chunks.activation_condition: When experiments are designed or interpreted to confirm the preferred hypothesis rather than genuinely testing whether it can be falsified

anchors.evidence_quote: Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer proposes a targeted pre-buy test to reduce uncertainty about demand faster than continued operation of the current product, applying the experimentation mechanism.

chunks.text: Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.
Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.
Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
Anchoring: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes how 'people focus too much on a single, specific piece of information (the anchor), often a precise number, to misleadingly influence overall judgment' — directly opposes FPA's mission.
chunks.activation_condition: When a precise number anchors judgment and misleadingly influences the overall assessment, even though the number's precision exceeds the reliability of the underlying data

chunks.text: Base Rates: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.

anchors.evidence_quote: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer requires defining directional operating thresholds upfront to avoid post-hoc rationalization disguised as precise decision-making.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes the direct opposition: experimentation 'requires intellectual humility and willingness to seek disconfirming evidence' while confirmation bias drives processing information 'to reach a desired conclusion'.
expansions.activation_condition: When experiments are designed or interpreted to confirm the preferred hypothesis rather than genuinely testing whether it can be falsified

expansions.substrate_chunk: Experimentation conflicts with confirmation-bias when tests are designed or interpreted to confirm the hypothesis rather than genuinely challenge it.
expansions.why_relevant: Experimentation conflicts with confirmation-bias when tests are designed or interpreted to confirm the hypothesis rather than genuinely challenge it.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Narratives can resist experimental revision by making the expected story feel truer than the observed result.
expansions.why_relevant: Narratives can resist experimental revision by making the expected story feel truer than the observed result.
expansions.affinity_rationale: From tensions — 'compelling stories about expected outcomes resist revision even when experimental results contradict the narrative', creating emotional attachment to pre-experimental beliefs.
expansions.activation_condition: When a compelling narrative about expected outcomes creates emotional resistance to accepting experimental results that contradict the story

expansions.substrate_chunk: Boundaries conflicts with optionality when prematurely fixed boundaries close off adjacent opportunities that emerge only through exploratory scope expansion.
expansions.why_relevant: Boundaries conflicts with optionality when prematurely fixed boundaries close off adjacent opportunities that emerge only through exploratory scope expansion.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Boundaries conflicts with systems-thinking when artificially narrow system boundaries hide feedback loops and interdependencies that cross the defined perimeter.
expansions.why_relevant: Boundaries conflicts with systems-thinking when artificially narrow system boundaries hide feedback loops and interdependencies that cross the defined perimeter.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Theory-induced-blindness undermines boundaries when a familiar frame dictates the scope instead of the real objective and evidence landscape.
expansions.why_relevant: Theory-induced-blindness undermines boundaries when a familiar frame dictates the scope instead of the real objective and evidence landscape.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Directly corrupts boundary placement — described as 'a direct challenge to the wise use of boundaries' where a familiar model dictates scope instead of the real objective and evidence landscape.
expansions.activation_condition: When scope boundaries are being drawn by a familiar theoretical framework rather than derived from the actual problem structure and available evidence

expansions.substrate_chunk: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with commitment-bias when the team keeps investing in the first bottleneck story instead of reassessing whether it is still binding.
expansions.why_relevant: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with commitment-bias when the team keeps investing in the first bottleneck story instead of reassessing whether it is still binding.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias corrupts theory-of-constraints by making the team protect its favorite bottleneck story instead of measuring the real cap.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias corrupts theory-of-constraints by making the team protect its favorite bottleneck story instead of measuring the real cap.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes confirmation bias as encouraging substitution of familiar frameworks for objective constraint identification
expansions.activation_condition: When teams substitute a familiar framework for objective identification of the actual binding constraint

expansions.substrate_chunk: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with optimization-theory when local utilization improvements in non-constrained steps create the illusion of progress.
expansions.why_relevant: Theory-of-constraints conflicts with optimization-theory when local utilization improvements in non-constrained steps create the illusion of progress.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
expansions.why_relevant: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes how 'people focus too much on a single, specific piece of information (the anchor), often a precise number, to misleadingly influence overall judgment' — directly opposes FPA's mission.
expansions.activation_condition: When a precise number anchors judgment and misleadingly influences the overall assessment, even though the number's precision exceeds the reliability of the underlying data

expansions.substrate_chunk: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.
expansions.why_relevant: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.

expansions.substrate_chunk: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with bayesian when avoidance of precise probability estimates prevents the quantitative prior-updating that Bayesian reasoning requires.
expansions.why_relevant: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with bayesian when avoidance of precise probability estimates prevents the quantitative prior-updating that Bayesian reasoning requires.

failure_hints.text: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
Experimentation theater can produce dashboards and learning language without changing the underlying decision.
Boundaries fail when they are drawn to protect comfort, politics, or convenience instead of the actual purpose of the work.
Premature narrowing can kill options and block the generation of better explanations or solutions.
Constraint language becomes theater when no current cap, governing metric, or movement evidence is named.
The model breaks when teams keep fixing the old bottleneck after the constraint has already shifted somewhere else.
Teams can use simplicity to hide uncertainty and present a rounded answer as if the underlying variance no longer matters.
Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.

heuristic_hints.text: Keep the experiment tied to a clear hypothesis and gather only enough facts to prove or disprove it.
Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.
Formalize the scope and expected output before the analysis sprawls.
Use boundaries to respect real cognitive limits by breaking work into genuinely manageable chunks.
Define constraints rigorously up front so the problem scope and the bottleneck candidate are explicit before solutioning.
Disaggregate and prioritize until only the branches affecting the binding constraint stay in focus.
Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.

premortem_hints.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
What do you think the other side doesn't get about your perspective?
What is this work actually supposed to cover, and what is deliberately out of scope?
Are these limits serving the goal, or are they mainly protecting us from difficult evidence or accountability?
Before committing: what is the single measured limit that would still cap throughput if every local improvement in this plan succeeded next week?
If this first bottleneck actually moves, what step becomes the next cap within two weeks?
If this was cut, would anything be lost?

select_when: Uncertainty can be reduced faster and cheaper through targeted tests than through longer debate, forecasting, or opinion escalation.
Each experiment can be tied to a clear hypothesis, success threshold, and downstream decision instead of being run for its own sake.
Reversible probes can inform larger irreversible commitments, reallocations, or product choices.
The team is stuck in argument about what is true, but a structured test could resolve the dispute.
danger_when: Experimentation has become theater, generating activity, dashboards, or post-hoc learning language without changing any real decision.
Local test wins are being overgeneralized while the team ignores system interactions, long-term effects, or behavior changes outside the test window.

select_when: A project, analysis, or negotiation needs explicit scope lines before effort spreads into unowned terrain.
The current work is overloaded because limits on responsibility, attention, or working-memory burden have not been set clearly enough.
A team needs to distinguish what is in frame, out of frame, and who owns each part before deeper analysis becomes useful.
The next useful move is to define constraints that protect purpose rather than let complexity expand by default.
danger_when: Scope lines are being drawn mainly to avoid inconvenient evidence, hard conversations, or accountability.
The inquiry is being narrowed so early that important options, causes, or adjacent realities will be filtered out before they are understood.

select_when: A conjunctive chain has a binding constraint, so improving non-binding stages will not raise throughput.
There are competing action options and the team can name the current bottleneck with a measured limit before ranking fixes.
Local optimization is masking the true leverage point because many high-quality improvements still leave key outcomes flat.
One blocking dependency, queue, approval step, scarce specialist, or handoff is visibly capping the whole delivery plan.
danger_when: The environment is too unstable for one fixed bottleneck frame because the limiting factor is moving every cycle.
The team is performing bottleneck theater and cannot name the governing metric, current cap, or evidence that the cap moved after intervention.

select_when: A decision needs directional clarity, bounded ranges, or operating thresholds more than an illusion of exact certainty.
Extra decimal places, caveats, or scenario branches are consuming attention without changing the decision that would actually be made.
The audience needs to act, align, or prioritize quickly and will be harmed more by cluttered precision than by a well-calibrated approximation.
The current explanation is accurate enough to sound rigorous but too overloaded to be useful for prediction, choice, or coordination.
danger_when: Simplicity is being used to hide uncertainty, skip the underlying analysis, or present a rounded answer as if the variance no longer matters.
The model is simplifying so far that the result becomes vague, slogan-like, or detached from the assumptions that make it usable.

frame_elements.element_text: There's a clear pivot we could do — we've had three customers tell us unprompted that they'd pay way more for a specific workflow tool we could build
frame_elements.evidence_quote: There's a clear pivot we could do — we've had three customers tell us unprompted that they'd pay way more for a specific workflow tool we could build
frame_elements.fragility_signal: customer signal fails pre-buy test or lacks commercial commitment

frame_elements.element_text: I'm trying to decide: pivot now while we have runway and a real signal, or push harder on the current product
frame_elements.evidence_quote: I'm trying to decide: pivot now while we have runway and a real signal, or push harder on the current product
frame_elements.fragility_signal: other pivots, hybrids, or non-pivot growth strategies exist

reframings.reframed_question: What would it take to test if those three customers would actually pay way more for the workflow tool before committing to the pivot?
reframings.what_opens: Path to validate the customer signal's commercial viability through a low-cost pre-buy test like a landing page or LOI, reducing risk of over-relying on unprompted feedback

reframings.reframed_question: How might a hybrid approach look that adds the workflow tool as a premium feature to the current product instead of a full pivot?
reframings.what_opens: Exploration of incremental integration preserving existing 22 customers and $4K MRR while capturing the new signal, avoiding all-or-nothing choice

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Assistant discusses sunk costs implicitly via runway but does not explicitly distinguish sunk costs from future costs, identify specific exit costs of pivot (e.g., opportunity cost of runway consumed), or value optionality of preserving current path vs committing to new product.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Recognizing lock-in to current customers/employees or runway burn could favor pushing current product longer, reversing pivot recommendation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Assistant identifies stakeholders (customers, CS employee, ex-co-founder) and gives communication scripts, but does not analyze power dynamics (e.g., employee resentment blocking morale, customer churn vetoing pivot) or specify influence strategies beyond honesty/notices.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Poor handling of employee or customer resistance could prevent pivot implementation, altering feasibility.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Assistant notes CS role goes away and runway constraint but does not identify displaced alternatives (e.g., what hiring CS deferred, runway for current vs pivot vs other uses) or compare marginal returns of pivot vs optimizing current product.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Reallocating engineer/time to current product optimizations could extend runway or growth without pivot risks.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Assistant distinguishes conversational vs commercial signal and proposes pre-buy test, but does not assess bias in the three customers (e.g., selection effects, unrepresentativeness of 22 customers), check base rates for dental SaaS pivots, or identify missing evidence like broader surveys.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: If signal is noise, no-pivot recommendation strengthens substantially.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Assistant sets 14-day decision date with pre-defined bar before tests, sequences tests/conversations first then commit/walk, addresses delay risks (runway shrink, employee uncertainty), recommends why this order (data before decision).

Assistant sizes downsides (test rejection kills hope, customer unhappiness, employee layoff), distinguishes mitigate (pre-buy test, transition plans, severance) vs adapt (honesty notices), addresses recovery (references, migration discounts).

Assistant identifies CS employee incentives (avoid busywork), ex-co-founder (passive but relationship risk), proposes alignment via advance notice/severance to prevent divergence (resentment, defection).

Assistant treats customer willingness as epistemic uncertainty reducible by pre-buy test/experiment, calibrates signal strength (conversational weak), matches to decision approach (test before pivot commitment).
Immaterial: No rivals, partners, or reacting parties mentioned in user question; assistant briefly notes selling customer list to competitor as rare option without modeling responses.

questions: What would it take for you to reverse course and return to the current dental SaaS product if the pivot to the workflow tool fails after 6 months?
Who specifically would need to be in place or compensated to make unwinding the pivot feasible with your 14 months runway?
What ongoing commitments, like contracts with your 22 customers, would make reversing the pivot more difficult than sticking with the current flat product?
Beyond the CS employee and former co-founder, which of your 22 paying customers would be most vocal or influential if you transition away from the current product?
Who among your two employees or external advisors needs to fully buy in versus just comply with the pivot decision and pre-buy test?
What level of agreement from the three customers who mentioned the workflow tool would you need before informing the former co-founder with her 15% equity?
With 14 months runway and two full-time employees, what other uses for the engineer's time during the next 90 days could support the current product instead of the pivot build?
If you keep the CS employee beyond a layoff, what specific tasks related to the 22 customers or workflow tool demand could she handle without creating busywork?
How would shifting burn rate or reallocating funds from the current $4K MRR maintenance change your runway if the pre-buy test fails?
What other data from your 22 customers, beyond the three conversational comments, might indicate demand for the workflow tool versus noise around the current flat product?
Which past signals about customer needs in dental practices have proven unreliable, and how does that affect trusting these three unprompted comments?
What additional evidence from prospects like the one who didn't convert would upgrade the conversational signal to something decision-grade before your 14-day deadline?

triage_scores.evidence: Faint signal in recommending inform former co-founder (friend-shareholder) out of courtesy to prevent resentment and preserve relationship, but does not distort pivot decision or ignore faults.

Imposes a strict 14-day decision deadline and pre-defined pass bar (e.g., 2/3 commit) after a quick pre-buy test this week, prematurely collapsing pivot uncertainty despite 14-month runway and only conversational signals from three customers, overriding potential for deeper validation.
Mentions runway shrinking without a decision date as a mild pressure point, but does not use crisis or adrenaline to distort or compress normal scrutiny.

Answer recommends informing CS employee and former co-founder upfront about pivot uncertainty and likely layoff 'before you've made the decision' and as 'courtesy' to prevent resentment, specifically overriding exclusion of former co-founder input while tying it to reciprocal relationship norms, despite query noting uninvolved status.

Overweights the vivid 'unprompted signal' from three customers (2 existing, 1 prospect) as 'real signal, not a weak one' and 'stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR', basing the entire pre-buy test and 2/3 pass bar recommendation on this tiny sample while omitting base rates like 22 customers' representativeness or historical pivot success rates at $4K MRR scale.

Answer minimizes pain of hard conversations by framing them as relieving for customers ('Your honesty won't surprise them; it will relieve them') and necessary to unlock decision, omitting deeper exploration of emotional fallout or failure risks while pushing founder to initiate them immediately.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Distinguishes between conversational interest and commercial commitment to assess signal strength accurately.
evidence_quotes: "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial.
Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes directly contrast weak conversational signals with strong commercial ones, establishing the need for higher validation before pivoting.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Proposes a concrete pre-buy test with defined criteria and timeline to validate demand empirically.
evidence_quotes: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?"
If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot.
Give yourself 14 days.
before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit?
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes specify the test mechanics, pass criteria, and deadline, forming a falsifiable experiment to de-risk the pivot.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Recommends structured transition options for existing customers to avoid pivot drag.
evidence_quotes: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate
The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes outline specific non-abandonment strategies and warn against common pitfalls, ensuring continuity without stalling the pivot.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Outlines tiered options for handling the CS employee, prioritizing transparency and fairness.
evidence_quotes: First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can)
Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected."
option three, then option one if the pivot confirms.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes present sequenced, humane options with specifics on communication and severance, balancing business needs with ethics.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Advises informing the former co-founder transparently without granting decision rights to preserve relationships.
evidence_quotes: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed

query:
Solo founder decides whether to pivot B2B SaaS product from current offering to new workflow tool amid flat growth, $4K MRR, 14 months runway, and 22 customers.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] Runway 14 months at current burn
- [ACTIVE] 22 paying customers on current product
- [ACTIVE] Two full-time employees (engineer, CS)
- [ACTIVE] Conversational signal from three customers (2 existing, 1 prospect), no price/timeline
- [ACTIVE] Former co-founder holds 15% equity, uninvolved 8 months

Original framing: Founder assumes pivot signal from three unprompted customer comments is strong; weighs pivoting now with 14 months runway vs pushing current flat product; excludes other pivots or non-pivot options.
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Run pre-buy test this week with the three customers at specific price ($X, 3 months prepay); define pass bar upfront (e.g., 2/3 commit); set 14-day decision deadline; if passes, pivot with transition plan for current customers (freeze/sunset/migrate), inform CS employee of uncertainty then likely layoff with severance, inform former co-founder without seeking input; have hard conversations first as they unlock the decision.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
A few things worth separating. "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. But I want to ask: are those three customers in your existing customer base, or are they prospects you've been pitching? And has the conversation gone past "we'd pay more for that" into "here's what I'd pay, here's when, here's what it would replace"?

The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial. Those two signals look the same on a gut level but they're different enough to drive the decision.

---

OK — that changes the weight. Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade. Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI. If they won't do that, the signal you have is "people like the idea of the thing" which is qualitatively different from "people will pay for the thing."

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.

---

Don't kill it — transition it. If you pivot, the 22 customers need a landing zone. Options: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate; or sell the customer list to a competitor, which is rare at your scale but not impossible in vertical SaaS.

The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot. You'll keep doing bug fixes and small features "just for them" and the pivot will take 50% longer than it should. Be honest with them early about what's happening and give them a clean option.

On your fear — it's real, and it's also the thing that often kills pivots. Founders delay the hard customer conversation because they're protecting their own hope. The customers know something is wrong when growth is flat for four months. Your honesty won't surprise them; it will relieve them.

---

You have three options and none of them are painless.

First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can), and a genuine reference and support in finding her next role. This is the most common answer and often the best one. You don't drag her through a project where she has nothing to do.

Second: pivot her role. Is there something she could do during the build period — market research, early pipeline development for the new product, partnership conversations? If the work is real and useful, this can work. If you're inventing busywork, she'll see through it immediately and it's worse than the layoff.

Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected. I want you to know so you can think about what you want to do. If you decide to look around, I'll support that and I'll still give you severance when the pivot happens." Some people appreciate the advance notice; others will leave immediately, which may or may not suit you.

My honest read: option three, then option one if the pivot confirms. She deserves to hear this before you've made the decision, not after. The fact that she left a stable job for this gives her the right to participate in knowing what's coming.

---

She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.

Tell her before you run the pre-buy test with the three customers. Frame it as: "I'm considering a pivot. Here's the signal. Here's what I'm going to test. I wanted you to know before I do it." You're not asking for permission; you're keeping her in the loop as someone who still owns part of the company.

This matters less for the pivot decision itself and more for your relationship with her. Passive shareholders who find out about major strategic decisions via email announcements get resentful, and friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends. Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem.

---

One thing. Set a decision date. Right now your list ends with "commit or walk" but doesn't have a when. Without a when, the list becomes three more months of "considering the pivot" while your runway shrinks and your employees wonder what's happening.

Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call. Not "we'll see how it feels" — an actual date on your calendar where you decide. The decision is easier when you've pre-committed to making it.

Also worth adding: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.

---

Of course. One last thing. The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working. Those conversations are the bottleneck, not the strategic question.

Have the conversations. The strategic answer will fall out of them. Good luck.
